updates.title: Claude Design gets enterprise upgrade: design system imports, Claude Code sync, canvas editing, and lower token use
updates.summary: Anthropic updated Claude Design to focus on enterprise brand compliance and design-to-development workflows. Key additions include design system imports from GitHub or file uploads, tighter Claude Code integration with shared components, direct canvas editing with stronger layout controls, more MCP tool connections for a smoother design-to-code handoff, and reduced token consumption. Claude Design now lives in the sidebar of the Claude desktop app and is also accessible at claude.ai/design.
updates.accuracy_notes: Details confirmed across multiple aggregator sources (Releasebot, vktr.com, TechRepublic, AIWeekly) and are consistent with prior Anthropic product trajectory. The official docs.anthropic.com page was inaccessible for direct verification (HTTP 403). Date of June 18 is consistent across sources.

Slide 5: Where to find it: claude.ai/design or the Claude desktop app sidebar

updates.title: Claude adds enterprise-managed MCP connector access: zero-touch Okta authorization for Team and Enterprise plans
updates.summary: Anthropic launched a beta for enterprise-managed MCP connector authorization, starting with Okta as the identity provider. Admins can now provision MCP connectors organization-wide once, and employees get automatic zero-touch access on first login — no repeated authorization prompts. The feature brings centralized auth across Claude Chat, Claude Code, and Claude Cowork for Team and Enterprise plans. MCP app partners supported at launch include Asana, Atlassian, Canva, Figma, Granola, Linear, and Supabase, with Slack coming soon. Early adopters Ramp reported 2,000 employees provisioned through Okta with zero manual steps.
updates.accuracy_notes: Confirmed across Releasebot, AIWeekly, Okta's own newsroom, the Model Context Protocol blog, and the Anthropic blog (claude.com/blog/enterprise-managed-auth). The Okta partnership is independently corroborated by Okta's press release. Date of June 19, 2026 confirmed. Feature is currently in beta.

updates.title: Your Claude Code limits are hidden until they're not, and this script surfaces them on every message
updates.summary: XDA Developers published a guide covering a community-built script that displays Claude Code's usage limits directly in the terminal on every message — fixing a frustrating visibility gap in the tool. By default, Claude Code only exposes usage data when you manually run /usage in the terminal, and on claude.ai you have to dig into account settings to find it. The script passively surfaces where you stand on limits with each response, giving developers early warning before hitting rate walls.
updates.accuracy_notes: Article is confirmed published on June 19, 2026 (search result indicated 'posted 2 hours ago'). Content is about a community workaround rather than an official Anthropic release; the script itself cannot be directly verified since the article URL returns HTTP 403. The underlying pain point (hidden limits) is well-corroborated by multiple prior user reports and the Anthropic Rate Limits XDA coverage. No accuracy red flags — topic is narrow and verifiable.

updates.title: Claude Code changelog: wheel scroll acceleration, model selection improvements, background session reliability, and billing fixes
updates.summary: The Claude Code changelog received updates including a new wheel scroll acceleration setting in the terminal, improvements to model selection and usage attribution, more reliable background session handling, and fixes to several billing and performance issues. These are incremental quality-of-life and reliability improvements rather than feature launches.
updates.accuracy_notes: Details sourced via search aggregators — exact date (June 18 vs 19) is estimated based on proximity to other confirmed June 18-19 release note entries. The official changelog URL was inaccessible (HTTP 403). These details appeared consistently in multiple search results describing the same batch of updates. Low risk of inaccuracy — changelog entries are factual and narrow.
